coredata.dc:description: Excessive Cu2+ is toxic to plants. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit certain chelating properties towards heavy metals, and they also demonstrate antioxidant activities. To explore the mechanism for alleviating the Cu2+ toxicity of Salvia miltiorrhiza Bunge mediated by CQDs, CQDs that contained CC, CO, H–O, C–N and C–O functional groups with particle size less than 10 nm and that emitted blue fluorescence were prepared. S. miltiorrhiza seedlings were treated with 200 μM of Cu2+ and 500 mg/L of CQDs to relieve stress. Exogenous CQDs effectively restored plant phenotype; reduced Cu2+, H2O2 and malondialdehyde contents and restored total superoxide dismutase, peroxidase and catalase activities under Cu2+ toxicity. Simultaneously, an association network of Cu2+ transport-related and metabolic pathway genes of phenolic acids and terpenoids was established on the basis of cross-species transcriptome analysis. Combined with reverse transcription quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction analysis, the potential molecular mechanism of CQDs, i.e. promoting phenolic acid biosynthesis to alleviate Cu2+ toxicity, was revealed by activating the expression of key enzyme genes of phenolic acid synthesis. This study provides a theoretical basis for Cu2+ pollution prevention and control in plants. It also laid a foundation for alleviating Cu stress by using CQDs in agricultural production.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive Cu2+ is toxic to plants. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit certain chelating properties towards heavy metals, and they also demonstrate antioxidant activities. To explore the mechanism for alleviating the Cu2+ toxicity of Salvia miltiorrhiza Bunge mediated by CQDs, CQDs that contained CC, CO, H–O, C–N and C–O functional groups with particle size less than 10 nm and that emitted blue fluorescence were prepared. S. miltiorrhiza seedlings were treated with 200 μM of Cu2+ and 500 mg/L of CQDs to relieve stress. Exogenous CQDs effectively restored plant phenotype; reduced Cu2+, H2O2 and malondialdehyde contents and restored total superoxide dismutase, peroxidase and catalase activities under Cu2+ toxicity. Simultaneously, an association network of Cu2+ transport-related and metabolic pathway genes of phenolic acids and terpenoids was established on the basis of cross-species transcriptome analysis. Combined with reverse transcription quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction analysis, the potential molecular mechanism of CQDs, i.e. promoting phenolic acid biosynthesis to alleviate Cu2+ toxicity, was revealed by activating the expression of key enzyme genes of phenolic acid synthesis. This study provides a theoretical basis for Cu2+ pollution prevention and control in plants. It also laid a foundation for alleviating Cu stress by using CQDs in agricultural production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The micronutrient copper (Cu) is a transition metal with atomic number 29, an atomic mass of 63.5 g mol−1 and a density of 8.96 g cm−3. It is the 25th most abundant chemical component in the Earth's crust and the 3rd most used worldwide (Faller and Hureau, 2009). It is an essential micronutrient for plant development, and it exists in the form Cu+ and Cu2+ under physiological conditions. Cu2+ acts as a structural element in regulatory proteins and participates in the electron transport chain of photosynthesis and respiration, oxidative metabolism, cell wall metabolism and hormonal signalling (Sarwar et al., 2019). Cu is absorbed in the form of Cu2+ or Cu chelate, and it exhibits certain fluidity in plants. Its concentration in the dry mass of plants is small, generally ranging from 2 mg kg−1 to 20 mg kg−1. However, its concentration in the dry mass of plants that varies between 20 mg kg−1 and 100 mg kg−1 is toxic to most plants (Sparks, 2023). Soil contamination by Cu2+ is largely caused by human actions, which are represented by industrial, mining and agricultural activities. The intensive use of Cu2+-containing agrochemicals or swine manure is the major source of Cu2+ entry into agricultural soil (Jensen et al., 2016). This scenario is raising concerns because world population is expected to reach 9 billion in 2050 (Sedgh-Gooya and Torki, 2022). At toxic levels in soil, Cu2+ reduces the absorption of water and mineral nutrients (Sedgh-Gooya and Torki, 2022), promotes oxidative stress (Nazir et al., 2019) and affects photosynthesis (Cambrollé et al., 2015), causing reduced growth (Tisarum et al., 2023) and plant production (Kumar et al., 2021). It has been reported that soil high Cu pollution is mainly concentrated in southern China. About 3.08% of the agricultural areas faced a high Cu risk, higher than the pollution rates of the whole land uses. In addition, a large number of agricultural land faced moderate or low Cu pollution in China, which seriously affected the safety and quality of crops (Liu et al., 2017). Cu pollution will also seriously affect Chinese herbal medicines. According to statistics, the Cu pollution rate of Chinese herbal medicines has reached 16.09% in China, seriously restricting their production and utilization (Zhao, 2014). Excessive Cu is absorbed by Chinese herbal medicines will seriously threaten human health through the food chain (Yang et al., 2023).

Salvia miltiorrhiza is one of the traditional Chinese herbal medicines, which belongs to the family Labiatae and the genus Salvia, has a long history of domestication and cultivation in China. Its major production areas are spread throughout southern and northern China, including, but not limited to, Sichuan, Shaanxi, Henan and Shandong provinces (Liang et al., 2020; Yao et al., 2022). The dry rhizomes of S. miltiorrhiza are rich in antioxidant active ingredients represented by phenolic acid, such as rosmarinic acid (RA), salvianolic acid A and salvianolic acid B (SAB), and tanshinone, including cryptotanshinone (CPT), tanshinone I (Tan I) and tanshinone IIA (Tan IIA), making them a natural plant-derived oxidative stress repair agent (Shi et al., 2019). The major pharmacological components of S. miltiorrhiza alleviate various diseases by mediating mitochondrial damage and repair, including, but not limited to, cardiovascular diseases (Li et al., 2018), cancer (Yan, 2016) and neurodegenerative diseases (Zhao et al., 2019). As a heavy metal-targeting mitochondrion, Cu2+ can activate endogenous secondary metabolic pathways to a certain extent by causing mitochondrial damage in plants represented by S. miltiorrhiza, endowing plants with a certain level of oxidative stress resistance from the perspective of chemical defence (Li et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2018). S. miltiorrhiza is used as a medicine through its dry roots or rhizomes. However, its medicinal parts are in direct contact with soil during growth and development, making this plant susceptible to Cu2+ pollution in soil. The Cu2+ content of S. miltiorrhiza should not exceed 20 mg/kg according to Chinese Pharmacopoeia (Chinese Pharmacopoeia Committee, 2020). However, the Cu2+ content of S. miltiorrhiza in different producing areas exceeded the standard, with an exceeding rate of 31.25%, which seriously restricts the safety and quality of S. miltiorrhiza (Meng et al., 2009). Therefore, it is necessary to explore approaches to mitigate Cu contamination in S. miltiorrhiza to ensure its quality and safety.

In recent years, innovations in nanotechnology have provided effective solutions to the challenges posed by abiotic stresses in plants (Khalid et al., 2022). A variety of nanomaterials have been used to alleviate Cu stress in plants (Alp et al., 2022; Xin et al., 2022). Among these, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been extensively studied (Ducic et al., 2021; Meena et al., 2021). CQDs are a new type of nanomaterial with carbon atoms as the skeleton structure and particle size less than 10 nm; they exhibit good fluorescence properties (Yuan et al., 2019). The surface of CQDs is rich in oxygen-containing groups, such as hydroxyl or carboxyl groups, making CQDs water-soluble and easy for plants to absorb (Tetsuka et al., 2012). Previous studies have reported that CQDs can form chelates with divalent metal ions, such as Cu2+, Ag2+ and Cd2+; it can also activate the antioxidant enzyme system of plants and promote the accumulation of secondary metabolites (Arumugam and Kim, 2018; Ducic et al., 2021). These characteristics have led to the widespread application of CQDs in research on alleviating plant stress caused by heavy metals. Studies had shown that CQDs effectively removed reactive oxygen species and free radicals in S. miltiorrhiza, alleviated the adverse effects of high-temperature stress, and had no impact on the quality and safety of the Chinese herbal medicines (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). However, it is not yet known whether CQDs had an alleviating effect on Cu stress in S. miltiorrhiza.

In this survey, we prepared CQDs that contained O–H, CO, CC, C–N and C–O functional groups, with particle size less than 10 nm, which could emit blue fluorescence. Cu2+-stressed S. miltiorrhiza seedlings were alleviated by applying CQDs, and the activities of antioxidant enzymes, Cu2+, phenolic acid and tanshinone contents of different treatment groups were determined. Furthermore, cross-species transcriptome analysis was conducted to screen genes that may be involved in Cu2+ transport, and reverse transcription quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) analysis was performed for Cu2+ transport-related proteins, phenolic acid and tanshinone key enzymes. This study aims to reveal the physiological and molecular mechanisms of S. miltiorrhiza in resisting Cu2+ toxicity by alleviating oxidative damage through an antioxidant enzyme system and secondary metabolites. It provides a reference for research on the anti-heavy metal pollution of S. miltiorrhiza and a theoretical basis for removing Cu2+ toxicity during cultivation. It also laid a foundation for the development and utilization of CQDs in alleviating Cu stress for crops in agricultural production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Preparation and performance characterization of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Refer to the previous methods (Lu et al., 2023). Step 1: The sample is dried to constant weight at 50 °C. Use a grinding bowl to grind them to powder. Step 2: Take powder and add it to the reaction kettle. Add distilled water, and place the reaction kettle in a vacuum drying oven. Heat it to 120 °C for 2 h before cooling it to room temperature; Step 3: Place the reaction kettle back into the vacuum drying oven and heat it to 180 °C. After 6 h of reaction, the sample was filtered by filter paper, then filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then subjected to dialysis for 48 h, and finally freeze-dried into dry powder. The fluorescence of CQDs was detected by UV imager (GenSens S2, CLINX) at 250 nm. The excitation wavelength of the fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent Technologies) was set to 365 nm, and the emission wavelength was set to 450 nm to detect the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. The surface groups of CQDs were detected by Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and the particle size was detected by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (F20 S-Twin, FEI Tecnai G2) (Markovic et al., 2023; Sadrolhosseini et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: S. miltiorrhiza seedlings were treated with Cu2+ and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: S. miltiorrhiza, the test material, originated from Zhongjiang County (31°N, 104°E), Sichuan Province, China. Referring to previous research (El-Beltagi et al., 2020; Riaz et al., 2022), use 0 μM CuSO4·5H2O as CK, 50, 100, 200, 400, 800 μM CuSO4·5H2O was used to irrigate the root of S. miltiorrhiza for 10 days, respectively. Discovered 200 μM CuSO4·5H2O is most suitable for the stress of S. miltiorrhiza. After root irrigation with CuSO4·5H2O at this concentration for 5 days, S. miltiorrhiza seedlings were treated with 0 mg/L CQDs (Corresponding volume of ddH2O) as CK, and root irrigation with 250, 500, 750, and 1000 mg/L CQDs for 5 days, respectively. It has been proven that 500 mg/L CQDs is the optimal concentration for alleviating copper stress in S. miltiorrhiza. Finally, with 0 μM CuSO4·5H2O + 0 mg/L CQDs (Corresponding volume of ddH2O) as CK, 200 μM CuSO4·5H2O (200 μM CuSO4·5H2O was first used to treat S. miltiorrhiza for 5 days, and then corresponding volume of ddH2O were used to treat for 5 days), 200 μM CuSO4·5H2O + 500 mg/L CQDs (200 μM CuSO4·5H2O was first used to treat S. miltiorrhiza for 5 days, and then 500 mg/L CQDs were used to treat for 5 days), and 500 mg/L CQDs were used to irrigate root of S. miltiorrhiza for 10 days, respectively, to explore the mechanism of CQDs in alleviating Cu2+ stress damage in S. miltiorrhiza. Five biological replicates were guaranteed for each treatment group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of the Cu2+ content
ce:section.ce:para: The root and overground of S. miltiorrhiza is dried at 50 °C to a constant weight, ground into a fine powder, and passed through a 20-mesh sieve. Accurately weigh 100 mg of the fine powder and place it into a digestion tube. Add 4 mL of nitric acid and 1 mL of perchloric acid, mix well, and digest for 12 h. Heat the mixture at 180 °C with a digester until the liquid volume is reduced to approximately 500 μL and the solution is completely clear and transparent. The Cu2+ content is then detected using ICP-MS, following previously established methods (De la Calle et al., 2017; Xing et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phenotypic and physiological data determination of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Determine the phenotypic data of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings in different treatment groups, including main root length, fibrous root length, root diameter, root fresh weight, overground length, overground fresh weight, and chlorophyll content (Riaz et al., 2022). Measure physiological data such as SOD, POD, and CAT activities, as well as MDA and H2O2 content (Yu et al., 2022; Zhong et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of metabolites of S. miltiorrhiza by HPLC
ce:section.ce:para: Collect the fresh roots and overground parts of S. miltiorrhiza, dry them to a constant weight at 50 °C, grind them into a fine powder, and pass the powder through a 20-mesh sieve. Accurately weigh 100 mg of the fine powder, place it in 10 mL of 80% methanol, and mix thoroughly. The extraction steps and HPLC conditions for rosmarinic acid (RA) and salvianolic acid B (SAB) were carried out according to previously established methods (Huang et al., 2019; Meihong et al., 2018). For the extraction and determination of tanshinone, 150 mg of S. miltiorrhiza fine powder was accurately weighed, placed in 50 mL 100% methanol, and mixed evenly. The extraction steps and HPLC conditions were also carried out according to previous methods (Bai et al., 2023; Li et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integration and analysis of the RNA-seq datasets
ce:section.ce:para: Collect RNA seq datasets of different species under Cu2+ exposure from the SRA database (Table 1 ). Subsequently, the original read data was cleaned, joints and low-quality bases were removed, and the read segment data was assembled and quantified using Kalisto software (Bray et al., 2016), referencing the genomes of the different species, quantification and differential expression analysis were performed for the RNA-seq. GO analysis was performed using the Blast2GO gene ontology analysis tools (http://www.blast2go.com) to determine the molecular function of all genes (Robles, 2005). KEGG pathway enrichment in quinoa using KOBAS online tool (Xie et al., 2011). Correlations between gene expression were calculated from the ggcorrplot package in R based on RNA-seq data and co-expression networks were constructed by Cytoscape (Shannon et al., 2003) with a screening threshold of P < 0.05, r < −0.8, or r > 0.8. Referring to previous reports, the Pearson correlation coefficient was used to calculate the correlation between gene expression (Liu et al., 2021; Wang and Auwerx, 2017). When drawing the co-expression network, the blue edge indicates a negative correlation and the red edge indicates a positive correlation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RT-qPCR analysis
ce:section.ce:para: RNA from root and overground parts of S. miltiorrhiza in different treatment groups was extracted using plant total RNA extraction kit (LABGENE, China), and reverse-transcribed into cDNA using TransScript® First-Strand cDNA Synthesis SuperMix (TransGen Biotech, China). Using Actin (Accession number: HM231319) and GAPDH (Accession number: KF234408) as internal reference genes, RT-qPCR analysis was performed for Cu2+ transport-related proteins, phenolic acid and tanshinone synthesis key enzymes (Liao et al., 2022; Zhong et al., 2024). Actin and GAPDH were both widely used as reference genes due to their extensive distribution and relatively constant expression levels in cells (Su et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2010). Primers used for RT-qPCR are shown in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistics
ce:section.ce:para: All data in this study were analysed using the Origin 2020 and SPSS statistics program, and the means were compared by the least significant difference (LSD) test at the 0.05 level of significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterisation of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In accordance with FTIR detection, the prepared CQDs have functional groups, such as O–H, CO, CC, C–N and C–O (Fig. 1 A). TEM revealed that the particle size of CQDs is less than 10 nm (Fig. 1B), indicating that these CQDs are carbon-based materials with a diameter of less than 10 nm. Interestingly, the obtained nanomaterials were excited with blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light. Full-wavelength scanning revealed that CQDs were excited at 365 nm and had an emission wavelength at 450 nm (Fig. 1C), signifying that the prepared CQDs exhibited fluorescence characteristics. Previous studies have shown that CQDs possess functional groups such as O–H, CO, and CC, which confer strong water solubility to CQDs. Their particle size of less than 10 nm allows them to be easily absorbed by plants and enter plant cells. Additionally, their strong photosensitivity and photosynthetic sensing characteristics can enhance plants' light utilization efficiency, thereby increasing the productivity of photosynthesis (Markovic et al., 2023; Pan et al., 2015; Ünlü et al., 2022). The CQDs synthesized in this study exhibit the typical characteristics of CQDs, demonstrating their feasibility for application in plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening of the optimal treatment concentration of CQDs for alleviating Cu2+ toxicity of S. miltiorrhiza
ce:section.ce:para: The growth and development of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings began to be gradually inhibited (particularly in the roots) with an increase in Cu2+ content. When Cu2+ concentration reached 800 μM, serious damage occurred in the overground parts and roots of the plants (Fig. S1A). When Cu2+ concentration was 200 μM, the main root length, lateral root length, root diameter, root fresh weight and overground fresh weight of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings significantly decreased by 23%, 40%, 19%, 64% and 28% compared with CK, respectively (Figs. S1B–S1G). Moreover, the contents of chlorophyll A and chlorophyll B decreased significantly (Figs. S1H and S1I), indicating that the seedlings of S. miltiorrhiza will be damaged but will not die, and the phenotype was evident at this concentration. In addition, 50 μM and 100 μM Cu2+ treatments did not obviously inhibit the characteristics of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings, whilst S. miltiorrhiza seedlings were damaged too seriously under 400 μM and 800 μM Cu2+ treatments and were unsuitable for subsequent experiments. Finally, 200 μM Cu2+ concentration treatment was selected for subsequent experiments.

For the alleviating effect of CQDs on Cu2+, the phenotype of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings began to recover gradually with an increase in CQD concentration (Fig. S2A), primarily indicating that main root length, lateral root length, root fresh weight and chlorophyll A and chlorophyll B contents increased significantly. This trend was most apparent when the concentration of CQDs reached 500 mg/L. However, when the concentration of CQDs exceeded 500 mg/L, the relief effect was gradually weakened, and 1000 mg/L of CQDs even inhibited the growth of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings (Figs. S2B–S2I). Therefore, 500 mg/L of CQDs was selected for follow-up experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phenotypic statistics of CQDs alleviating Cu2+ toxicity in S. miltiorrhiza seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with CK, 200 μM Cu2+ treatment inhibited the growth and development of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings (particularly the roots), 200 μM Cu2++500 mg/L CQD treatment restored the plant phenotype, and 500 mg/L CQD treatment alone effectively promoted the root development of S. miltiorrhiza (Fig. 2 A). The main root length, lateral root length, root diameter, root fresh weight, aboveground part length and chlorophyll A and chlorophyll B contents of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings decreased significantly under 200 μM Cu2+ treatment compared with CK. The aforementioned phenotypes were restored to the level close to CK in the 200 μM Cu2++500 mg/L CQD treatment group. When 500 mg/L of CQDs was applied alone, the main and lateral root lengths of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings were significantly higher than CK, and no significant difference was found in other phenotypes (Fig. 2B–I). High concentrations of Cu2+ stress damaged cells and inhibited plant growth and development (Riaz et al., 2022; Yuce et al., 2024). However, the application of CQDs enhanced photosynthetic efficiency, thereby promoting plant growth and development, increasing Cu tolerance, and restoring the phenotype.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes in Cu2+ content of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings treated with Cu2+ and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Under the treatment of 200 μM of Cu2+, Cu2+ content of the roots and overground parts of S. miltiorrhiza increased significantly. After exogenous application of CQDs, Cu2+ content was significantly reduced in the overground parts and roots. The Cu2+ content of the roots and overground parts of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings treated with 500 mg/L of CQDs alone was significantly lower than those in the 200 μM Cu2+ and 200 μM Cu2++500 mg/L CQD treatment groups, and presented no significant difference with CK (Fig. 3 A and B). The transfer coefficient of Cu2+ from the roots to the overground parts was significantly higher in the 200 μM Cu2+ treatment group than in the others, the 200 μM Cu+500 mg/L CQD treatment exhibited no significantly difference with CK, and the 500 mg/L CQD treatment alone was significantly lower than CK (Fig. 3C). These results suggest that CQDs can reduce the absorption and transport of Cu2+ in S. miltiorrhiza, and this phenomenon may be caused by the chelation of Cu2+ by CQDs (Ducic et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs alleviate Cu2+ toxicity by activating the antioxidant oxidase system of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: The non-enzymatic antioxidant system of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings was determined. First, 200 μM of Cu2+ stress significantly promoted the accumulation of H2O2 in the overground parts and roots, and the accumulation of malondialdehyde (MDA) in the roots of S. miltiorrhiza. In the 200 μM Cu2++500 mg/L CQD treatment group, the H2O2 content of the overground parts and roots and the MDA content of the roots were significantly decreased. In addition, the application of 500 mg/L of CQDs alone significantly promoted the accumulation of H2O2 in the overground parts, but the content was significantly lower than that in the 200 μM Cu2+ treatment group, and exhibited no effect on the accumulation of H2O2 in the roots and MDA in the overground parts and roots (Fig. 4 A and B). The antioxidant enzyme activity of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings in different treatment groups was further determined. Firstly, 200 μM of Cu2+ significantly promoted the activities of total superoxide dismutase (T-SOD) and catalase (CAT) in the overground parts and roots, and promoted the activities of peroxidase (POD) in the roots. In the 200 μM Cu2++500 mg/L CQD treatment group, the activities of T-SOD, POD and CAT in the roots and overground parts were significantly lower than those in the 200 μM Cu2+ treatment group. The application of 500 mg/L of CQDs alone significantly promoted POD and CAT activities in the roots (Fig. 4C–E). When plants were subjected to toxicity or stress, their cells were damaged, leading to the release of large amounts of reactive oxygen species (ROS) or free radicals. In response, their antioxidant enzyme systems, such as T-SOD, POD, and CAT, were activated to eliminate the ROS and free radicals. Therefore, these physiological indicators often increased significantly under stress conditions (Wang et al., 2024). The cells of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings were damaged under Cu2+ toxicity, and a large number of ROS and free radicals were released. Plants activate their own antioxidant enzyme system to remove ROS and free radicals. Exogenous CQDs can activate the antioxidant enzyme system of plants without damaging plant cells, reducing plant damage caused by Cu2+ toxicity, decreasing the contents of MDA and H2O2 and the activities of T-SOD, POD and CAT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs alleviated Cu2+ toxicity by promoting phenolic acid accumulation in S. miltiorrhiza seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Phenolic acid and tanshinone, the two major active components of S. miltiorrhiza, exhibit good antioxidant activity and free radical scavenging ability (Chen et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2008). Treatment with 200 μM of Cu2+ significantly promoted the accumulation of RA and SAB in the roots and overground parts of S. miltiorrhiza seedlings. In the 200 μM Cu2++500 mg/L CQD treatment group, the RA and SAB contents of the roots and overground parts were significantly reduced compared with those in the 200 μM Cu2+ treatment group. The application of 500 mg/L of CQDs alone resulted in the significant up-regulation of RA content in the roots and SAB content in the roots and overground parts compared with CK (Fig. 5A, B and S3A). Treatment with 200 μM of Cu2+ resulted in a significant decrease of CPT content and Tan I, but exerted no significant effect on Tan IIA in the roots of S. miltiorrhiza (tanshinones accumulated only in the roots of S. miltiorrhiza) (Fig. 5C and S3B). These results indicated that phenolic acid rather than tanshinone was used to remove active oxygen and free radicals in S. miltiorrhiza seedlings damaged by Cu2+ toxicity. Moreover, the application of CQDs can promote the biosynthesis of phenolic acid and accelerate the repair of Cu2+ stress damage, and thus, the content of phenolic acid in the 200 μM Cu2++500 mg/L CQDs treatment group was significantly decreased compared with that in the 200 μM Cu2+ treatment group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Association network between Cu2+ transport-related genes and phenolic acid, terpenoid metabolic pathway genes were analysed on the basis of cross-species transcriptomics
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the evolutionary and adaptive mechanisms of biological responses to Cu2+ virulence from a global perspective of coregulatory networks, we collected and collated RNA-Seq data that covered more than 50 samples and 10 GB from multiple public databases. They came from six eukaryotes at different evolutionary stages, including fungi (Saccharomyces cerevisiae), animals (Drosophila melanogaster and Mus musculus) and plants (Arabidopsis thaliana, Vitis vinifera and S. miltiorrhiza). Data cleaning, normalisation correction, reference genome-based assembly and quantification of all collected resulting expression matrices were conducted, resulting in the final transcriptome expression profiles of the six species under Cu2+ toxicity (Fig. 6 A). We performed a gene ontology (GO) enrichment analysis of the differentially expressed genes of the six species under Cu2+ toxicity and explored the functional modules shared by different species and unique to each species (Fig. 6B). Overall, six conserved Cu2+ response functional modules attracted our attention, which are as follows: small molecule metabolic processes (GO: 0044281), redox activity (GO: 0016491), redox processes (GO: 0055114), cellular periphery (GO: 0071944), amino acid metabolism processes (GO: 0009072) and carbon metabolism processes (GO: 0005975). These results suggest that the carbon cycle, represented by amino acid metabolism, may maintain endohomeostasis under Cu2+ toxicity by regulating redox processes within an organism.

To dig deeper into the conserved Cu2+ toxicity-response network of plants and animals and the core genes involved in network regulation, we specifically analysed transcriptional dynamics modules from M. musculus, A. thaliana and S. miltiorrhiza under Cu2+ exposure (Fig. 7 A–C). Functional enrichment analysis of 686 differentially expressed genes shared by Cu2+ exposure (Fig. 7D) showed their primary localisation at the plasma membrane and involvement in biological processes, such as redox reaction, transmembrane transport, phenolic acid metabolism, terpenoid metabolism and stress response (Fig. 7E). We extracted one-to-one direct orthologous single-copy genes from the shared differentially expressed gene set of three species for their functional and pathway classification. Furthermore, we examined the transcript level correlations amongst key genes in Cu ion transport, phenolic acid metabolism and terpenoid metabolism pathways, demonstrating that most Cu2+ transporter genes exhibited significant positive correlation with the expression levels of phenolic acid and terpenoid metabolism genes, whilst only a few genes presented potential negative regulatory relationships amongst them (Fig. 7F).

In particular, we sampled transcript levels amongst genes related to Cu ion transport, phenolic acid metabolism and terpenoid metabolism in 1001 A. thaliana natural variant populations and transcriptomic datasets induced by different environmental factors from S. miltiorrhiza. Surprisingly, the COPT1 transporter gene showed a significant negative correlation with metabolic pathway genes in A. thaliana and a significant positive correlation with metabolic pathway genes in S. miltiorrhiza. However, the Nramp6 and the metabolic pathway genes were all significantly negatively correlated, and no significant correlation was found between the YSL2 and HMA1 genes and the expression patterns of the metabolic pathways (Fig. 7G). COPTs were a family of Cu-specific transport proteins in plants that primarily mediated the absorption of Cu2+ from the soil and its transport from the roots to the aerial parts of the plant. In S. miltiorrhiza, COPT1 showed a significant positive correlation with genes related to the phenolic acid metabolism pathway, indicating a connection between phenolic acid biosynthesis and Cu2+ transport. This explained the significant increase in the content of Cu2+, RA, and SAB in both the roots and aerial parts of S. miltiorrhiza under 200 μM Cu2+ treatment. In contrast, the expression pattern of COPT1 was negatively correlated with genes related to the phenolic acid metabolism pathway in A. thaliana. This difference was due to the distinct phenolic acid metabolic pathways between the two plants: A. thaliana synthesized tocopherol via the shikimate pathway, while S. miltiorrhiza produced RA and SAB via the tyrosine or phenylalanine metabolic pathway (Riewe et al., 2012; Tian et al., 2022). Although Nramps, YSLs, and HAMs also participated in the transport of heavy metals in plants, their expression patterns related to genes in the phenolic acid metabolism pathway in S. miltiorrhiza were not positively correlated or showed low correlation, possibly due to their lack of specificity for Cu transport.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RT-qPCR analysis revealed the potential molecular mechanism of CQDs in reducing Cu2+ toxicity and transport
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with CK, the 200 μM Cu2+ treatment significantly increased the expression levels of SmCOPT1 and SmCOPT2 in S. miltiorrhiza root, whilst significantly decreasing the expression levels of SmNramp6 and SmYSL2. These results indicated that S. miltiorrhiza absorbed Cu2+ in soil mostly by activating the expression levels of SmCOPT1 and SmCOPT2. In addition, SmCOPT2 was highly expressed in the overground parts, indicating that this gene can also mediate the transfer of Cu2+ from the roots to overground parts. The application of 500 mg/L of CQDs alone significantly down-regulated the expression levels of SmCOPT1 and SmCOPT2 in the overground parts. The expression levels of SmCOPT1 in the roots and SmCOPT2 in the overground parts were significantly decreased in the 200 μM Cu2++500 CQD treatment group (Fig. 8 A). These results indicated that the application of exogenous CQDs could inhibit the expression of the two genes, reducing the absorption of Cu2+ in soil and its transport to the overground parts.

The expression levels of SmTAT1, SmC4H, SmRAS and SmCYP98A14 in the roots and overground parts were significantly increased after 200 μM Cu2+ treatment. Meanwhile, SmHPPR and Sm4CL2 were significantly down-regulated in the roots and overground parts, and SmPAL was significantly down-regulated in the overground parts. This result indicates that S. miltiorrhiza largely promotes phenolic acid synthesis by activating the expression levels of SmTAT1, SmC4H, SmRAS and SmCYP98A14 to alleviate Cu2+ toxicity. The application of 500 mg/L of CQDs alone resulted in the high expression levels of SmTAT1 and SmC4H in the overground parts of S. miltiorrhiza, the high expression levels of SmPAL and SmRAS in the overground parts and roots and the high expression of Sm4CL2 in the roots, indicating that CQDs can activate these genes and promote the synthesis of phenolic acid. The application of exogenous CQDs can effectively reduce the toxic damage of Cu2+ by activating the synthesis of phenolic acids, such that S. miltiorrhiza does not need to synthesise more phenolic acids to alleviate toxicity. Therefore, in the 200 μM Cu2++500 CQD treatment group, the expression levels of some phenolic acid key enzyme genes were significantly decreased compared with those in the 200 μM Cu2+ treatment group (Fig. 8B).

The expression levels of genes related to tanshinone synthesis were further analysed in different treatment groups, and only the expression levels of SmMDC and SmCYP76A were significantly down-regulated under 200 μM Cu2+ treatment (Fig. 8C). This result is consistent with the trend of composition determination data in the current study. CQDs were again demonstrated to alleviate Cu2+ toxicity by activating phenolic acids rather than tanshinone biosynthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, CQDs that contained CC, CO, H–O, C–N and C–O functional groups with particle size less than 10 nm and that emitted blue fluorescence were prepared. CQDs can alleviate Cu2+ toxicity by activating antioxidant enzyme activity and promoting phenolic acid accumulation in S. miltiorrhiza seedlings. Cross-species integrated transcriptome analysis revealed the close correlation between Cu2+ transport-related genes and phenolic acid metabolic pathway genes. Combined with RT-qPCR analysis, we revealed the potential molecular mechanism of CQDs, i.e. promoting phenolic acid biosynthesis and alleviating Cu2+ toxicity by activating the expression of key enzymes in phenolic acid synthesis (Fig. 9 ). This work laid a theoretical foundation for the study of plant resistance to Cu2+ stress and the prevention and control of Cu2+ pollution. It also laid a foundation for alleviating Cu stress by using CQDs in agricultural production.
